upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, moist surface layer with a surface temp of 11°C and a tight 1.4°C spread at 1018mb, indicating near-saturation at the surface. The lapse rate from surface to 850mb is only -1.97°C/km — extremely stable, far below even moist adiabatic (~6.5°C/km) — confirming a strong low-level inversion that will severely cap thermal development. Winds back from E/SE at the surface to NW aloft (320° at 850mb, 290° at 700mb), suggesting a decoupled boundary layer; thermal ceiling is effectively the BL height reported in models (300–465m AGL) and convective potential is essentially zero (CAPE=0 everywhere).
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KHAF is reporting strong NNW winds 15–18kt gusting to 20kt, well-established sea breeze driving coastal flow. KRHV shows light W/NW 5–9kt at 21Z with scattered clouds at 8,000ft — benign inland conditions. KSFO calm to light NW, high thin broken layer at 20,000ft. KAPC light SW 6kt, KWVI light SW 7kt. No significant aviation hazards; coastal gradient between KHAF (NNW 18kt) and inland (KRHV 5kt W) confirms active sea breeze penetration.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NE-facing launch, valley thermal/sea breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 works on a combination of valley-driven thermals and the afternoon NW sea breeze that funnels through Niles Canyon. Ideal winds are NW to NNW at 8–15kt at launch; the site can handle a bit more given the ridge elevation. Thermals are the primary lift engine in the morning; ridge soaring becomes viable as the sea breeze fills in.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is wind direction: surface winds are tracking NNE to NE (23–42°) throughout the entire flyable window, which is a cross-to-tail direction for the NW-facing aspects of this ridge. HRRR and GFS are in full agreement — 10m winds sit at 12–16kt NNE all day. The boundary layer is also extremely shallow (90–465m), and the stable sounding (lapse rate -1.97°C/km) confirms thermals will be weak and capped low. Cloud cover goes to 100% by 2 PM, further suppressing heating. While wind speeds are within range, the NE direction creates a problematic cross-wind and potential rotor in the lee of the ridge. The 80m wind showing 15–23kt NNE adds turbulence risk above launch elevation.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 12–16kt from 23–41° NNE, a cross-to-tailwind for launch. BL height only 90–335m. Not viable — wrong direction and BL too shallow.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Direction marginally backs toward N (36–42°) but remains NNE. Speeds 13–16kt. BL grows slightly to 390–435m but cloud cover spikes to 58–100%, killing thermal development.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% cloud cover from 2 PM onward with 11–13kt NNE. BL stable at 435–465m. Cross-wind conditions persist; no thermal trigger available. No viable window.

verdict.hazards: Cross-to-tail wind direction all day; stable inversion caps BL under 465m; 100% overcast from 2 PM; potential rotor in lee of ridge

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-hill launch, NW-facing, used in moderate sea breeze
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch at Ed Levin is primarily a thermal/sledder site used when upper launches are too strong or conditions are building. It needs a mild NW to N wind and developing thermals to work. It's often used as a training hill or in the early afternoon sea breeze cycle.
sites.todaySetup: Same problematic NNE wind direction affects the 600ft launch as the upper launches. HRRR shows 15–18kt from 10–36° (NNE to NE) all morning through midday — this is a tailwind or strong crosswind depending on exact launch orientation. Speeds are actually higher here at 600ft than at 1750ft during the morning (15–18.5kt vs 12–16kt), likely a terrain channeling effect. The BL is marginally deeper here at times (up to 450m) but the moist, stable sounding prevents meaningful thermal development. 100% cloud cover locks in from 2 PM. No window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 15–18kt from 10–31° NNE/NE — elevated and cross/tail for this launch. BL only 105–360m. Definitely not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 15–18kt from 30–36°, still NNE. BL around 355–440m. 13–66% cloud cover. Wrong direction, borderline speeds, no safe window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, 13–14kt from 32–35°. Conditions do not improve. No viable window.

verdict.hazards: Strongest surface winds of the three Ed Levin launches due to terrain funneling; wrong direction; stable capped BL; full overcast PM

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, used for beginner/training flights and light-wind days
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft training hill works best with light NW winds 5–10kt and stable, smooth conditions. It's primarily a sled-ride site for students or a short-flight thermal day when conditions are too light uphill. It needs on-cycle direction — N to NW — and calm enough air for student safety.
sites.todaySetup: The 300ft hill is the anomaly today: morning winds are actually from 347–358° (nearly due N) at only 5–7kt, which is an acceptable on-cycle direction for this launch. This looks like the valley morning drainage flow before the sea breeze fills in. However, HRRR shows a sharp and dramatic wind shift at noon — jumping abruptly from 5kt N to 15.7kt from 31° NNE, a classic and unpredictable sea breeze front arrival. This transition is the key hazard: the timing is uncertain and the shift is abrupt. Before noon the winds may be acceptable for experienced P2s, but the incoming sea breeze surge around midday creates a sharp and hazardous transition. Post-noon the hill is exposed to 10–12kt NNE cross-winds with 100% overcast. Not recommended given the sea breeze front timing risk.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light N winds 5–7kt from 347–358° — technically on-cycle and within range. BL 95–455m. Potentially usable for experienced P2s as a sledder only, but the sea breeze front lurks.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Abrupt wind shift at noon to 15.7kt from 31° NNE — the sea breeze front hits hard. This transition is dangerous and unpredictable in exact timing. Do not launch after 10:30 AM.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 10–12kt NNE cross-wind with 100% cloud cover. Thermals shut down. No viable window.

verdict.wind: 5–7 kts N (AM only); shifts to 15+ kts NNE at noon
verdict.hazards: Sharp sea breeze front arrival near noon — abrupt wind shift from 5kt N to 15kt NNE; must land well before front arrives; light morning winds may be too weak for meaningful lift
verdict.skillLevel: P2+ (experienced only, tight early window, commit to landing before 10:30 AM)

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing, ridge lift from ocean sea breeze
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site powered by the NW to W sea breeze off the Pacific. Ideal winds are W to NW at 12–20kt at ridge level. The site requires enough onshore flow to generate clean ridge lift along the cliff face; too light and you can't stay up, too strong or too N and rotor risk increases below the ridge.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is marginal today due to insufficient and variable onshore flow. The HRRR shows morning winds nearly calm to light easterly (1–5kt from 88–294°) — essentially offshore — which is the exact opposite of what this site needs. KHAF METARs are showing a strong NNW flow (15–18kt gusting 20kt) just a few miles south, but the model is not capturing that at the site grid point until mid-afternoon when winds back NW/N at 6–7kt. The buoy data (Half Moon Bay 46012: 17.5kt at 310°, SST 12.4°C; SF Bar 46026: 15.6kt at 320°) confirms a robust marine push, but the cool SST (11.6–12.4°C) and 2.5m wave height suggest a deeper, more persistent marine layer that is keeping the coastal flow compressed and possibly causing a local shadow at the Mussel Rock grid point. The 12–13 PM window shows winds going nearly calm again (5kt from 358° then N) before settling NW at 6–7kt by 3–6 PM — too light and inconsistent for reliable ridge soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Nearly calm to light easterly 2–5kt from 88–97° — offshore component. Unsuitable. Marine layer likely holding at the cliff, BL only 85–405m.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind direction transitions from E to N to NW but speeds remain 4–6kt, with 9% cloud cover at noon suggesting partial clearing. Too light for reliable ridge lift; rotor risk below cliff during direction transitions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW to NNW flow develops at 6–7kt with 100% cloud cover returning by 2 PM. KHAF's 18kt NNW suggests real marine push nearby, but model shows site getting only a fraction of that. Marginally flyable for experienced pilots if actual on-site wind exceeds 10kt.

verdict.bestWindow: 2 PM – 5 PM (NW flow arrives, but verify on-site)
verdict.wind: 6–7 kts NNW (model); actual may be higher per KHAF obs
verdict.hazards: Light and variable winds; offshore component in AM; 100% overcast PM; cool SST deepens marine layer; rotor risk during direction shifts; KHAF obs suggest more wind than model at cliff face — verify on-site before committing

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge, SW-facing, thermal/sea breeze combo site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a thermal and ridge site that works on the afternoon NW sea breeze funneling through the Fremont plain. Ideal winds are NW to WNW at 10–18kt at the 2,517ft summit. It's an east Bay inland site that relies on solar heating to drive thermals and the classic afternoon gradient wind.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same unfavorable NNE wind regime as Ed Levin today. HRRR and GFS are fully aligned: 10m winds run 12–16kt from 23–42° NNE all day, which is a crosswind or even slightly behind the SW-facing launch aspects. The 80m winds are even stronger (15–23kt NNE), confirming the flow is not just a surface artifact. The strongly stable sounding (-1.97°C/km lapse rate, CAPE=0) eliminates any meaningful thermal development. Cloud cover hits 100% from 2 PM. There is no window where both direction and thermal development align for this site today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 12–16kt from 23–41° NNE. Cross/tail wind for SW-facing launch. BL 90–335m. Thermals weak and capped. Not viable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 14–16kt from 36–42° NNE persists. BL grows to 390–435m but cloud cover is variable (15–58%). Direction remains wrong throughout.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast locks in by 2 PM, 11–13kt NNE. No solar heating, no thermal trigger, wrong direction. No viable window.

verdict.hazards: Persistent NNE cross/tail wind all day; stable inversion prevents meaningful thermals; 100% overcast from 2 PM; 80m winds 15–23kt add turbulence risk at summit

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Friday looks like a significant change but with a new problem: 100% cloud cover all day per both HRRR and GFS, and winds calm out in the morning (3–10kt from NNE early, collapsing to near calm by noon). The sea breeze does not appear to fill in meaningfully. Without solar heating to break the overcast or generate thermals, and with virtually no gradient wind, this site is effectively socked in and dead. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 16kt from 19° at 2 PM, suggesting some NNE flow persists under the overcast. No fly.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar overcast story to 1750ft. HRRR shows 13kt NNE at 8 AM collapsing to 2–5kt variable by midday, then weak WNW sea breeze at 5–7kt by late afternoon. 100% cloud cover all day. Tomorrow.io shows light N winds gusting to 14kt. The clouds prevent any thermal development. The late-day WNW shift is directionally better, but without solar energy this site won't produce. No fly.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: 100% overcast all day, winds light and variable (4–7kt from variable directions, eventually backing WNW by afternoon). No thermal trigger available under the overcast. Wind speeds marginally acceptable but direction inconsistent and lift absent. No viable window.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Friday is cloudy (100% all day) with wind initially from SE/E (offshore) in the morning, transitioning to weak W/WNW by afternoon (3–5kt). The offshore morning component combined with persistent overcast makes this a poor coastal day. Tomorrow.io shows near-calm to 5kt from variable directions. Without a clear onshore push, ridge lift will be insufficient. No fly.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Friday brings 100% overcast all day with winds dropping to near calm by midday (3kt from 14° at noon) before shifting to a weak WNW/NW by afternoon. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 15kt from NE mid-afternoon, hinting at some NE drainage persisting. The full overcast eliminates thermal potential entirely. No fly.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday (March 28) shows improvement: winds shift to SW-S in the morning (5kt from 194–223°) then veer WNW by afternoon (5–8kt from 295–317°). BL height reaches 1,105m by 2 PM — the highest of the 4-day window — suggesting thermals may finally break through the stable pattern. Cloud cover is a concern (56–100% PM), but the 2 PM BL height with WNW flow hints at a viable afternoon thermal/ridge window. Marginal but watch this day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday similar to 1750ft with WNW flow developing by afternoon at 5–8kt and BL heights reaching 1,105m by 2 PM. Morning is light SW (3kt). Better directional alignment than today. Could be marginal flyable P2 window in the 1–3 PM slot if clouds allow some solar heating.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday morning light SW (3kt) shifting WNW by afternoon. BL expands significantly to 1,105m by 2 PM. If clouds partially clear midday, this could be a decent thermal training day with light WNW flow. Worth watching.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Saturday shows W-WSW flow (245–293°) at 4–8kt with mixed cloud cover (48–88% afternoon). The onshore component is better aligned for ridge soaring, and BL heights of 415–470m suggest some thermal mixing. If actual ridge-face winds reach 10–12kt, this could be a marginal soaring day. Watch KHAF obs on the morning of the 28th.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Saturday's WNW flow at 3–5kt and BL up to 1,065m by 2 PM is more encouraging than today. However cloud cover is still 45–100% in the afternoon. The light wind speeds may be insufficient for reliable soaring, but thermals could be workable if heating breaks through. Marginal P2/P3 day — needs on-site verification.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Sunday (March 29) looks like the best day of the outlook: morning near calm (0.8kt), then WNW sea breeze develops to 4–6kt by afternoon with BL heights reaching 825–1,070m. Cloud cover clears significantly (0–5% in morning and afternoon, though 100% at noon is odd and may be a model artifact). Zero CAPE throughout. Light winds may limit ridge soaring but thermals could work in a classic valley thermal cycle if clearing allows. P2-friendly if winds fill in. 1% precip at 6 PM worth watching.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Sunday similar — near calm to WNW 3–6kt, BL to 1,070m, mostly clear bookends (0% AM and PM cloud, 100% at noon again). Light thermal day potential. Worth flying if the midday cloud clears as quickly as the afternoon 6% cloud cover suggests.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Sunday's calm morning and light WNW afternoon breeze with the highest BL height of the week (1,070m at 4 PM) makes this a potentially good thermal training day. Clear AM and PM with possible midday cloud. Keep an eye on 1% precip flag at 6 PM.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Sunday shows mixed signals — near calm morning with 1% precip flags all day, variable wind directions (N to W), and BL heights 200–515m. The precip flags and light variable winds make this uncertain. If the precip risk is residual and clears early, the afternoon could see WNW flow. Low confidence — monitor Sunday morning buoy and KHAF obs.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Sunday is the best day for Mission Peak in the outlook: near calm AM, light WNW by afternoon (2–4kt at 10m, 7–8kt at 80m suggesting stronger flow aloft), BL to 1,130m — the deepest of the week. Clear AM and afternoon. Thermal development possible if surface heating connects to the BL. Light at-launch winds may be the limiting factor; the 80m wind showing 7kt suggests better flow just above ridgeline. Worth checking.

watchlist: NNE wind regime dominating all Bay Area inland sites today — Ed Levin and Mission Peak fully shut down; classic post-frontal or offshore flow pattern
Ed Levin 300ft: sea breeze front arriving abruptly near noon — anyone launching the morning window must land by 10:30 AM
Mussel Rock: KHAF reporting 15–18kt NNW while model shows only 6kt at site — on-site verification critical before committing to afternoon session
Cool SST (11.6–12.4°C) at SF Bar and Half Moon Bay buoys indicates deep, persistent marine layer — coastal clearing may be delayed
Weekend (Saturday–Sunday) showing improvement with WNW flow and deeper BL heights — best flying days of the 4-day window, especially Sunday
tldr: Today is basically a bust for paragliding — NNE winds are cross-to-tail at Ed Levin and Mission Peak all day, and Mussel Rock is too light and variable. The only sliver is Ed Levin 300ft before 10:30 AM for experienced P2s willing to catch a short sled before the sea breeze front hits. Eyes on the weekend: Saturday and especially Sunday look like the best flying days of the week as WNW flow returns and the BL finally opens up.
